title_en: Discourse on the Essentials of Diagnosis and the Ending of the Channels
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: What are the essentials of diagnosis? Qibo replied: In the first and second months, the qi of heaven is just beginning to open out and the qi of earth is just beginning to stir; the qi of the person is in the liver. In the third and fourth months, the qi of heaven is fully opened and the qi of earth is settled and active; the qi of the person is in the spleen. In the fifth and sixth months, the qi of heaven is abundant and the qi of earth rises high; the qi of the person is in the head. In the seventh and eighth months, the yin qi begins its killing work; the qi of the person is in the lung. In the ninth and tenth months, the yin qi begins to freeze and the qi of earth begins to close up; the qi of the person is in the heart. In the eleventh and twelfth months, the ice is at its height and the qi of earth is sealed shut; the qi of the person is in the kidney.

Therefore, in spring, needle the scattered transport points and the interstices of the flesh, stopping as soon as blood appears; in severe cases move the qi through, in mild cases let it merely circulate. In summer, needle the connecting transport points, stopping as soon as blood appears; when the qi is exhausted the circulation closes off, and painful conditions will certainly subside. In autumn, needle the skin, following its natural creases, applying the same method above and below, stopping when the spirit shows a change. In winter, needle the transport openings at the interstices of the flesh; in severe cases go straight down, in mild cases disperse the needling downward. Spring, summer, autumn, and winter each have their proper needling, following the method appropriate to where the qi resides.

If in spring one needles at the summer level, the pulse becomes disordered and the qi grows faint, the needling penetrates and spreads into the bone marrow, the disease cannot be cured, and it causes the person to lose all appetite and also to become short of qi. If in spring one needles at the autumn level, the sinews will contract and cramp, rebellious qi will circulate about causing coughing, the disease will not be cured, and it will cause the person to be frequently startled and to weep. If in spring one needles at the winter level, evil qi will lodge in the viscera, causing the person to become bloated; the disease will not be cured, and moreover the person will feel a constant urge to talk. If in summer one needles at the spring level, the disease will not be cured, and it will cause the person to become listless and weak. If in summer one needles at the autumn level, the disease will not be cured, and it will cause the person's heart to want to say nothing, apprehensive as though someone were about to seize him. If in summer one needles at the winter level, the disease will not be cured, and it will cause the person to be short of qi and often inclined to anger. If in autumn one needles at the spring level, the disease will not subside, and it will cause the person to be startled and eager to do something, then rise and forget what it was. If in autumn one needles at the summer level, the disease will not subside, and it will cause the person to favor lying down more and more, and to dream frequently. If in autumn one needles at the winter level, the disease will not subside, and it will cause the person to shiver periodically with cold. If in winter one needles at the spring level, the disease will not subside, and it will cause the person to want to lie down yet be unable to sleep, and when sleep does come, to have visions. If in winter one needles at the summer level, the disease will not be cured, the qi will rise, and various impediment disorders will develop. If in winter one needles at the autumn level, the disease will not subside, and it will cause the person to be frequently thirsty.

Whenever needling the chest and abdomen, one must avoid the five viscera. If the needle strikes the heart, death occurs within a full day's cycle; if it strikes the spleen, death occurs in five days; if it strikes the kidney, death occurs in seven days; if it strikes the lung, death occurs in five days; if it strikes the diaphragm, this counts as injury to the center, and although the disease may seem to heal, within a year the patient must die. To needle while avoiding the five viscera, one must know what is contrary and what is compliant. What is called compliant refers to the true location of the diaphragm and of the spleen and kidney; one who does not know this does the opposite. When needling the chest and abdomen, one must first lay cloth padding over the area, then needle through the single layer of cloth; if the needling does not bring about a cure, needle again. In needling one must be solemn and careful; when needling a swelling, rock the needle; in needling along the channels, do not rock it — this is the way of needling.

Huangdi said: I wish to hear how the twelve channels come to their end. Qibo said: When the taiyang channel comes to its end, the eyes roll upward, the body arches backward in convulsions and spasms, the complexion turns white, and profuse sweat breaks out — once it breaks out, death follows. When the shaoyang channel comes to its end, the ears go deaf, all the joints go slack, the eyes stare fixedly as the vessel connecting to them is severed; once that connection is severed, death occurs within a day and a half, and before death the complexion first turns greenish-white. When the yangming channel comes to its end, the mouth and eyes twitch, the person is easily startled and speaks deliriously, the complexion turns yellow, the channels above and below become exuberant, numbness sets in, and then the end comes. When the shaoyin channel comes to its end, the face turns black, the teeth appear elongated and grimy, the abdomen becomes distended and blocked, and above and below cease to communicate, and then the end comes. When the taiyin channel comes to its end, the abdomen becomes so distended and blocked that breathing is impossible, the person belches and vomits repeatedly; if vomiting occurs, the qi rebels upward and the face turns red, and if it does not rebel, above and below cease to communicate, whereupon the face turns black, the skin and body hair wither, and the end comes. When the jueyin channel comes to its end, there is heat within and dryness of the throat, the person urinates frequently and the heart is vexed; in severe cases the tongue curls up and the testicles retract, and then the end comes. These are the ways in which the twelve channels fail.

title_en: Discourse on the Essentials and Subtleties of the Pulse
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: What is the method of diagnosis? Qibo replied: The method of diagnosis should always be carried out at dawn, when the yin qi has not yet stirred and the yang qi has not yet dispersed, before food or drink has been taken, when the channels are not yet exuberant, the connecting vessels are even and regular, and the qi and blood are not yet in disorder — only then can one properly diagnose a pulse that has become abnormal. Feel the movement and stillness of the pulse, observe the clarity of the eyes, examine the five colors, note whether the five viscera show excess or insufficiency, whether the six bowels are strong or weak, and whether the body's form is flourishing or in decline; by weighing and correlating all of this, one determines the boundary between death and life.

Now the pulse is the storehouse of the blood: when it is long, the qi is well regulated; when it is short, the qi is diseased; when it is rapid, the heart is vexed; when it is large, the disease is advancing; when it is exuberant above, the qi rises high; when it is exuberant below, there is distension; when it is intermittent, the qi is failing; when it is fine, the qi is scanty; when it is rough, there is heart pain. When the pulse arrives turbid and forceful like a gushing spring, the disease is advancing and the complexion is ruined; when it departs faint and lingering as though a bowstring had snapped, death follows. The clarity of the eyes and the five colors are the outward bloom of the qi. Red should resemble white silk wrapped around cinnabar, not resemble ochre; white should resemble a goose feather, not resemble salt; green should resemble the lustrous sheen of a dark jade disc, not resemble indigo; yellow should resemble fine gauze wrapped around realgar, not resemble yellow earth; black should resemble layered lacquer, not resemble dull grey earth. When the subtle, failing form of the five colors becomes visible, the person's life will not be long. The clarity of the eyes is that by which one sees the myriad things, distinguishes white from black, and discerns short from long. If long is mistaken for short and white is mistaken for black, this shows that the essence is failing.

The viscera are the guardians of the interior. When the interior is overfull and the viscera are engorged, and the qi overwhelms the person so that he is fearful and distressed, his voice will sound as though he were speaking from inside a room — this is dampness in the internal qi. When speech is faint and the person can only speak again after a whole day has passed, this is qi that has been lost. When a person cannot keep his clothes and coverings gathered about him, and speaks indiscriminately of good and bad without regard for kin or stranger, this is derangement of the spirit and its clarity. When the granary cannot store what is given to it, this is because its gate does not hold firm. When the water spring does not stop, this is because the bladder cannot store. One who retains this guarding power lives; one who loses it dies. The five viscera are the strength of the body. The head is the storehouse of clarity and essence; when the head hangs low and the gaze sinks inward, the essence and spirit are about to be lost. The back is the storehouse of the chest; when the back is bent and the shoulders droop, that storehouse is about to collapse. The waist is the storehouse of the kidney; when a person can no longer turn and sway it, the kidney is about to be exhausted. The knees are the storehouse of the sinews; when a person can no longer bend and extend them, and walks stooped and leaning on support, the sinews are about to be exhausted. The bones are the storehouse of the marrow; when a person can no longer stand for long, and walking brings on trembling and shaking, the bones are about to be exhausted. If this strength is retained, one lives; if it is lost, one dies.

Qibo said: When the pulse runs contrary to the four seasons, an excess where there should be little is called essence-excess, and an insufficiency where there should be abundance is called dissipation. Where there should be excess but there is instead insufficiency, this too is called essence-excess; where there should be insufficiency but there is instead excess, this too is called dissipation. When yin and yang fail to correspond properly to each other, the disease is named blockage and rejection. Huangdi said: How does the pulse move together with the four seasons? How does one know where a disease is located? How does one know how a disease will change? How does one know when a disease has suddenly moved to the interior, and how does one know when it has suddenly moved to the exterior? I wish to ask about these five matters — may I hear your explanation? Qibo said: Let me speak of how the pulse turns together with the great workings of heaven. Beyond the myriad things and within the six directions, amid the changes of heaven and earth and the responses of yin and yang: the warmth of spring becomes the heat of summer, and the sternness of autumn becomes the fury of winter. The pulse rises and falls together with these four transformations: in spring it corresponds to the compass, in summer to the carpenter's square, in autumn to the balance beam, and in winter to the steelyard weight. Therefore, forty-five days after the winter solstice, yang qi begins to rise slightly and yin qi begins to descend slightly; forty-five days after the summer solstice, yin qi begins to rise slightly and yang qi begins to descend slightly. Yin and yang have their proper times, which correspond to the pulse as to a fixed schedule; when the schedule and the pulse fail to match, one can know how the pulse has diverged, and since this divergence has its own timing, one can thereby know the time of death. The subtlety lies in the pulse, and it must never go unexamined. Examining it has its guiding principles: begin from yin and yang; this beginning has its regularity, arising from the five phases; this arising has its measure, suited to the four seasons. In supplementing and draining, never lose sight of this, and be as one with heaven and earth. Grasping the sense of this oneness, one can thereby know death and life. Thus the voice corresponds to the five notes, the complexion corresponds to the five phases, and the pulse corresponds to yin and yang. From this we know: when yin is exuberant, one dreams of wading through great waters in terror; when yang is exuberant, one dreams of great fires blazing; when yin and yang are both exuberant, one dreams of killing and mutual injury. When qi is exuberant above, one dreams of flying; when exuberant below, one dreams of falling. When extremely full from eating, one dreams of giving things away; when extremely hungry, one dreams of taking things. When liver qi is exuberant, one dreams of anger; when lung qi is exuberant, one dreams of weeping. When short parasites abound, one dreams of crowds gathering; when long parasites abound, one dreams of fighting and mutual injury. Therefore there is a proper way to hold the pulse: emptiness and stillness of mind are what preserve its accuracy. In spring the pulse floats, like a fish swimming beneath the waves; in summer it lies at the skin, brimming over as though the myriad things had abundance; in autumn it sinks below the skin, like hibernating insects about to withdraw; in winter it lies at the bone, like hibernating insects sealed tightly away, while the noble person stays indoors. Hence it is said: one who understands the interior presses the pulse and takes careful note of it; one who understands the exterior follows it from end back to beginning. These six points are the great rules for holding the pulse.

When the heart pulse beats hard and long, the disease should manifest as a curled tongue with inability to speak; when it is soft and scattered, the disease should resolve of itself within a cycle. When the lung pulse beats hard and long, the disease should manifest as spitting blood; when it is soft and scattered, the disease should manifest as profuse sweating that even now will not stop recurring. When the liver pulse beats hard and long, and the complexion is not green, the disease should manifest as though from a fall or a blow, with blood collecting below the ribs and causing the person to pant with rebellious qi; when it is soft, scattered, and of lustrous color, the disease should be overflow-drink, in which the person becomes suddenly very thirsty and drinks excessively, so that the fluid easily penetrates the muscles, skin, and the regions beyond the intestines and stomach. When the stomach pulse beats hard and long, and the complexion is red, the disease should manifest as a fracture of the thigh; when it is soft and scattered, the disease should manifest as food impediment. When the spleen pulse beats hard and long, and the complexion is yellow, the disease should manifest as shortness of qi; when it is soft, scattered, and dull in color, the disease should manifest as swelling of the lower leg, resembling a condition of water retention. When the kidney pulse beats hard and long, and the complexion is yellow and red, the disease should manifest as a wrenched lower back; when it is soft and scattered, the disease should manifest as scantiness of blood that even now will not be restored. Huangdi said: When in diagnosis one finds the heart pulse tense, what disease is this, and what does it look like? Qibo said: The disease is called heart mounting; there should be a palpable mass in the lower abdomen. Huangdi said: On what grounds do you say this? Qibo said: The heart is a yang viscus, and the small intestine serves as its envoy; hence I say there should be a palpable mass in the lower abdomen. Huangdi said: When in diagnosis one finds the stomach pulse, what does the disease look like? Qibo said: When the stomach pulse is in excess, there is distension; when it is deficient, there is diarrhea.

Huangdi said: What is meant by a disease forming and then transforming? Qibo said: Wind disorder, once formed, becomes cold-heat; heat-jaundice, once formed, becomes wasting-thirst; reversal, once formed, becomes disease of the vertex; prolonged wind becomes diarrhea with undigested food; wind lodged in the vessels becomes a severe skin disease. The transformations of disease are too numerous to count. Huangdi said: All the various swellings and abscesses, contractures of the sinews, and pains in the bones — from what do these arise? Qibo said: These are swellings caused by cold qi, transformations brought about by the eight winds. Huangdi said: How does one treat them? Qibo said: These are diseases of the four seasons; treat them using what overcomes the offending qi, and they will be cured. Huangdi said: When an old disease stirs activity within the five viscera and thereby injures the pulse and complexion, how in each case can one know whether the disease is long-standing or has arrived suddenly? Qibo said: How thorough is this question! When one finds the pulse diminished but the complexion not lost, this is a new disease. When one finds the pulse not lost but the complexion lost, this is a long-standing disease. When one finds both the pulse and the five colors lost, this too is a long-standing disease. When one finds neither the pulse nor the five colors lost, this is a new disease. When the liver pulse and kidney pulse arrive together, and the complexion is greenish-red, the disease should stem from injury; if no blood has yet appeared, then once blood does appear, it will be as damp as though soaked in water.

Within both sides of the cubit position lie the free ribs; the outer aspect of the cubit position reflects the kidney, the inner aspect reflects the abdomen. Just above this, on the left: the outer aspect reflects the liver, the inner aspect reflects the diaphragm; on the right: the outer aspect reflects the stomach, the inner aspect reflects the spleen. Above this again, on the right: the outer aspect reflects the lung, the inner aspect reflects the chest; on the left: the outer aspect reflects the heart, the inner aspect reflects the membrane surrounding the heart. The front reflects the front of the body, the back reflects the back of the body. What is uppermost, at the very top, pertains to matters of the chest and throat; what is lowermost, at the very bottom, pertains to matters of the lower abdomen, lower back, thighs, knees, shins, and feet.

When the pulse is coarse and large, this is insufficiency of yin and excess of yang, producing heat within. When it comes rapidly and departs slowly, replete above and empty below, this is reversal disease of the vertex; when it comes slowly and departs rapidly, empty above and replete below, this is aversion to wind. Hence when a person is struck by pathogenic wind, it is the yang qi that receives it. When the pulse is sunken, fine, and rapid throughout, this is shaoyin reversal; when it is sunken, fine, rapid, and scattered, this is cold-heat disorder; when it is floating and scattered, this causes dizziness and collapse. All pulses that are floating without agitation belong to yang and indicate heat; those that are also agitated indicate disease in the hands. All pulses that are fine and sunken belong to yin and indicate bone pain; those that are also still indicate disease in the feet. A pulse that beats rapidly with one intermittent beat indicates disease in the yang channels, with diarrhea and stool containing pus and blood. In all abnormal cases, upon palpation: a rough pulse indicates excess of yang qi, a slippery pulse indicates excess of yin qi. Excess of yang qi produces fever of the body without sweating; excess of yin qi produces profuse sweating with a cold body; excess of both yin and yang together produces cold without sweating. When one presses outward and the pulse is felt inside but not outside, there is accumulation in the heart and abdomen. When one presses inward and the pulse is felt outside but not inside, there is heat in the body. When one presses upward and the pulse is felt above but not below, the lower back and feet are cold. When one presses downward and the pulse is felt below but not above, there is pain in the head and neck. When one presses down to the bone and the pulse qi is scanty, there is pain in the lower back and spine, together with impediment in the body.

title_en: Discourse on the Qi Manifestations of the Healthy Person
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: What is the pulse of a healthy person like? Qibo replied: In a person, with one exhalation the pulse beats twice, and with one inhalation the pulse also beats twice; over a complete breath, counting the pause between breaths, the pulse beats five times — this is called a healthy person. A healthy person is one who is free of disease. One should always use a person free of disease to regulate the diagnosis of a sick person; since the physician himself is not ill, he uses his own settled breathing to regulate the count for the patient — this is the rule. When a person's pulse beats only once with each exhalation and once with each inhalation, this is called insufficiency of qi. When a person's pulse beats three times with each exhalation and three times with each inhalation and is agitated, with heat at the cubit position, this is called warm disease; if the cubit is not hot but the pulse is slippery, this is called wind disease; if the pulse is rough, this is called impediment. When a person's pulse beats four times or more with each exhalation, this indicates death; when the pulse is cut off and fails to arrive, this indicates death; when the pulse is at times sparse and at times rapid, this indicates death.

The constant qi of a healthy person is endowed by the stomach; the stomach is the constant qi of a healthy person. When a person lacks stomach qi, this is called rebellion, and one in rebellion dies. In spring, when the stomach pulse is slightly wiry, this is called healthy; when it is very wiry with little stomach qi, this is called liver disease; when it is purely wiry with no stomach qi at all, this indicates death; when there is stomach qi together with a hair-like floating quality, this indicates a disease due in autumn, and if the hair-like quality is pronounced, the disease is present now. The true qi of the viscus is dispersed through the liver, which stores the qi of the sinews and membranes. In summer, when the stomach pulse is slightly hook-like, this is called healthy; when it is very hook-like with little stomach qi, this is called heart disease; when it is purely hook-like with no stomach qi at all, this indicates death; when there is stomach qi together with a stone-like sunken quality, this indicates a disease due in winter, and if the stone-like quality is pronounced, the disease is present now. The true qi of the viscus penetrates through the heart, which stores the qi of the blood and vessels. In late summer, when the stomach pulse is slightly soft and weak, this is called healthy; when it is very weak with little stomach qi, this is called spleen disease; when it is purely intermittent with no stomach qi at all, this indicates death; when soft and weak together with a stone-like quality, this indicates a disease due in winter, and if the weakness is pronounced, the disease is present now. The true qi of the viscus moistens the spleen, which stores the qi of the muscles and flesh. In autumn, when the stomach pulse is slightly hair-like, this is called healthy; when it is very hair-like with little stomach qi, this is called lung disease; when it is purely hair-like with no stomach qi at all, this indicates death; when hair-like together with a wiry quality, this indicates a disease due in spring, and if the wiry quality is pronounced, the disease is present now. The true qi of the viscus rises up to the lung, by which it circulates the nutritive and defensive qi, and yin and yang. In winter, when the stomach pulse is slightly stone-like, this is called healthy; when it is very stone-like with little stomach qi, this is called kidney disease; when it is purely stone-like with no stomach qi at all, this indicates death; when stone-like together with a hook-like quality, this indicates a disease due in summer, and if the hook-like quality is pronounced, the disease is present now. The true qi of the viscus descends to the kidney, which stores the qi of the bones and marrow. The great connecting vessel of the stomach is called Xu Li; it passes through the diaphragm and connects with the lung, emerging below the left breast, where its beating can be felt through the clothing — this is the pulse of the ancestral qi. If it is exuberant, panting, and frequently intermittent, the disease is in the interior; if it is knotted and moves crosswise, there is an accumulation; if it is cut off and fails to arrive, this indicates death. When the beating below the breast can be felt through the clothing, this shows that the ancestral qi is leaking away.

To know the excess and insufficiency of the cun kou pulse at the wrist: when the cun kou pulse felt beneath the hand is short, this indicates headache; when it is long, this indicates pain in the feet and shins; when it is hurried and strikes upward, this indicates pain in the shoulders and back. When the cun kou pulse is sunken and hard, this indicates disease in the interior; when it is floating and exuberant, this indicates disease on the exterior. When it is sunken and weak, this indicates cold-heat together with abdominal masses and pain in the lower abdomen. When it is sunken and moves crosswise, this indicates accumulation below the ribs, with pain from a crosswise accumulation in the abdomen. When it is sunken and racing, this indicates cold-heat. When the pulse is exuberant, slippery, and hard, this indicates disease on the exterior; when it is small, replete, and hard, the disease is in the interior. When the pulse is small, weak, and rough, this is called a long-standing disease; when it is slippery, floating, and rapid, this is called a new disease. When the pulse is tense, this indicates abdominal masses with pain in the lower abdomen. A slippery pulse indicates wind; a rough pulse indicates impediment; a moderate and slippery pulse indicates heat in the interior; an exuberant and tight pulse indicates distension.

When the pulse follows yin and yang appropriately, the disease is easy to cure; when the pulse runs contrary to yin and yang, the disease is difficult to cure. When the pulse accords with the proper sequence of the four seasons, it is said that the disease presents no complications; when the pulse runs contrary to the four seasons, or fails to correspond well to its governing viscus, it is said to be difficult to cure.

When there are many bluish-green vessels visible on the arm, this indicates loss of blood. When the cubit pulse is moderate and rough, this is called the languor-and-weakness syndrome. When a person lies quietly at ease yet the pulse is exuberant, this indicates loss of blood. When the cubit skin is rough but the pulse is slippery, this indicates excessive sweating. When the cubit skin is cold and the pulse is fine, this indicates diarrhea. When the cubit skin is coarse and constantly hot, this indicates heat in the interior.

When a diseased liver pulse appears on a gengxin day, death follows; when a diseased heart pulse appears on a rengui day, death follows; when a diseased spleen pulse appears on a jiayi day, death follows; when a diseased lung pulse appears on a bingding day, death follows; when a diseased kidney pulse appears on a wuji day, death follows. This is what is meant by saying that whenever the true, unmediated visceral pulse appears without any admixture of stomach qi, death always follows.

When the vessels of the neck pulsate visibly together with panting and rapid coughing, this indicates water disease. When the area beneath the eyes is slightly swollen, resembling a reclining silkworm, this indicates water disease. When the urine is yellow-red and the person prefers to lie quietly, this is jaundice. When a person feels hungry again immediately after eating, this is stomach jaundice. Swelling of the face indicates wind. Swelling of the feet and shins indicates water disease. Yellowing of the eyes indicates jaundice.
In a woman, when the shaoyin pulse of the hand beats vigorously, this indicates pregnancy.

The pulse may be contrary to or compliant with the four seasons even before any visceral pattern has appeared: if in spring or summer the pulse is thin, or if in autumn or winter the pulse is floating and large, this is called being contrary to the four seasons. When there is wind-heat yet the pulse is quiet, when there is diarrhea or blood loss yet the pulse is replete, when the disease is in the interior yet the pulse is deficient, when the disease is on the exterior yet the pulse is rough and hard — in all these cases the disease is difficult to treat; this too is called running contrary to the four seasons.

A person takes water and grain as the root of life; therefore if a person is cut off from water and grain, death follows, and if the pulse lacks stomach qi, death also follows. What is meant by lacking stomach qi is that only the true, unmediated visceral pulse is obtained, without any stomach qi present. What is meant by the pulse failing to obtain stomach qi is, for instance, a liver pulse with no wiry quality at all, or a kidney pulse with no stone-like quality at all. When the taiyang pulse arrives, it is surging, large, and long. When the shaoyang pulse arrives, it is at times rapid and at times sparse, at times short and at times long. When the yangming pulse arrives, it is floating, large, and short. Now, the healthy heart pulse comes strung together bead upon bead, like fingering a string of fine jade beads — this is called a healthy heart pulse; in summer, stomach qi is its root. The diseased heart pulse comes panting and continuous, slightly curved in its course — this is called heart disease. The dying heart pulse comes curved in front and stationary behind, like grasping a curved hook — this is called death of the heart. The healthy lung pulse comes languid and fluttering, like elm pods falling — this is called a healthy lung pulse; in autumn, stomach qi is its root. The diseased lung pulse comes neither rising nor falling, like stroking a chicken's feather — this is called lung disease. The dying lung pulse comes like a floating object, like wind blowing through hair — this is called death of the lung. The healthy liver pulse comes soft, weak, and swaying, like raising the tip of a long pole — this is called a healthy liver pulse; in spring, stomach qi is its root. The diseased liver pulse comes full, replete, and slippery, like stroking a long pole — this is called liver disease. The dying liver pulse comes tense and increasingly taut, like a newly strung bowstring — this is called death of the liver. The healthy spleen pulse comes harmonious and gentle, with its beats set apart, like a hen treading the ground — this is called a healthy spleen pulse; in late summer, stomach qi is its root. The diseased spleen pulse comes replete and rapidly full, like a hen lifting its foot — this is called spleen disease. The dying spleen pulse comes sharp and hard like a crow's beak, like a bird's spur, like a leaking roof, like flowing water — this is called death of the spleen. The healthy kidney pulse comes panting and strung together like a hook, firm when pressed — this is called a healthy kidney pulse; in winter, stomach qi is its root. The diseased kidney pulse comes like pulling on a vine, growing firmer under pressure — this is called kidney disease. The dying kidney pulse comes bursting forth like a snapped rope, striking abruptly like a flicked stone — this is called death of the kidney.
